diagrams.title: Digital Signature Creation Process
diagrams.description: A step-by-step block diagram showing how a digital signature is created from a plaintext message
elements: Message Input (Plaintext Document)
Hash Function (SHA-256) - shown as a processing box
Message Digest/Hash Output - shown as a compressed data block
Private Key - shown as a key icon
Encryption Process (RSA/ECDSA) - shown as an encryption box
Digital Signature Output - shown as a signed document icon with seal
Arrows showing flow: Message → Hash Function → Hash + Private Key → Encryption → Digital Signature
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the complete process of generating a digital signature, understanding how hashing and encryption combine to create authentication

diagrams.title: Digital Signature Verification Process
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing how a recipient verifies the authenticity of a received digital signature
elements: Received Message (Plaintext)
Received Digital Signature
Hash Function (SHA-256) - shown as processing box
Public Key of Sender - shown as key icon
Decryption Process - shown as decryption box
Comparison Logic (Two hash values) - shown as diamond shape
Verification Result (Valid/Invalid) - shown as checkmark or X mark
Arrows showing: Message → Hash Function → Hash1; Signature + Public Key → Decryption → Hash2; Hash1 and Hash2 → Comparison
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how verification works by comparing the decrypted hash with a newly computed hash, establishing the core security principle of digital signatures

diagrams.title: Asymmetric Key Cryptography in Digital Signatures
diagrams.description: A diagram showing the relationship between sender's key pair and the verification process in blockchain
elements: Sender's Private Key - kept secret, shown in locked vault
Sender's Public Key - publicly available, shown as accessible key
Message/Document being signed
Digital Signature created with private key
Blockchain Network showing public key distribution
Verification using public key
Arrows showing: Private Key → Signing; Public Key → Verification; Bidirectional relationship between key pair
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the fundamental principle of asymmetric cryptography - how a private key creates a signature that only corresponding public key can verify, critical for blockchain transaction security

diagrams.title: Classification of Digital Signature Types
diagrams.description: A hierarchical diagram showing different types of digital signatures and their relationships
elements: Digital Signatures (Main Category)
Symmetric Digital Signatures - shown with single key icon
Asymmetric Digital Signature Algorithms - shown with key pair
RSA Algorithm - most widely used
DSA (Digital Signature Algorithm) - US government standard
ECDSA (Elliptic Curve DSA) - used in Bitcoin/Ethereum
ECDSA secp256k1 - blockchain specific
ED25519 - modern high-speed algorithm
Arrows showing parent-child relationships
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the taxonomy of digital signature schemes, understanding which algorithms are used where (especially ECDSA in blockchain)

diagrams.title: Digital Signature in Blockchain Transaction
diagrams.description: A diagram showing how digital signatures secure blockchain transactions
elements: Transaction Creator (Sender) - shown as user icon
Transaction Data (inputs, outputs, amount) - shown as data block
Sender's Private Key - from wallet
Digital Signature Generation - signing transaction hash
Signed Transaction - broadcast to network
Mining Nodes/Validators - receiving transaction
Public Key Recovery from Signature
Verification using recovered public key
Blockchain ledger update
Arrows showing transaction flow and signature verification at each node
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how digital signatures are applied in real blockchain transactions, ensuring only rightful owners can spend cryptocurrency

charts.title: Digital Signature Creation Flowchart
charts.content: START → Input Message → Apply Hash Function → Generate Hash Digest → Encrypt Hash with Sender's Private Key → Generate Digital Signature → Append Signature to Message → END. This flowchart shows the sequential process from original message to signed message ready for transmission.

charts.title: Digital Signature Verification Flowchart
charts.content: START → Receive Signed Message → Separate Message and Signature → Apply Hash Function to Message → Decrypt Signature using Sender's Public Key → Extract Original Hash → Compare Both Hashes → IF Match: Valid Signature (Accept); IF No Match: Invalid Signature (Reject). This decision flowchart shows the binary verification outcome.

charts.title: Digital Signature vs Traditional Handwritten Signature
charts.content: Comparison dimensions: Security (Digital: Cryptographically secure, tamper-evident vs Traditional: Easily forgeable), Verification (Digital: Mathematical proof, instant vs Traditional: Expert analysis required), Storage (Digital: Easy to store and retrieve vs Traditional: Physical documents needed), Non-repudiation (Digital: Strong, cannot deny signing vs Traditional: Weak, can claim forgery), Cost (Digital: Low, electronic storage vs Traditional: High, paper and processing), Speed (Digital: Instantaneous vs Traditional: Time-consuming).

charts.title: Digital Signature Algorithms Comparison
charts.content: RSA: Key sizes 1024-4096 bits, widely compatible, slower signatures, used in SSL/TLS. DSA: Key sizes 1024-3072 bits, faster than RSA, US government standard, cannot encrypt. ECDSA: Key sizes 160-256 bits, very compact, fast computation, used in Bitcoin/Ethereum. Ed25519: 256-bit keys, fastest, most secure, modern standard, used in modern blockchain platforms.

branches: Creation Process (Hashing + Encryption with Private Key)
Verification Process (Hashing + Decryption with Public Key)
Cryptographic Primitives (Hash Functions, Asymmetric Encryption, Key Pairs)
Blockchain Applications (Transaction Signing, Smart Contracts, Identity)
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of digital signatures from creation to blockchain applications, helping students see how individual concepts interrelate in the broader security landscape

branches: Transaction Security (Ensures only owner can spend funds)
Wallet Architecture (Private key storage, signing transactions)
Public Key Cryptography (Key pairs, address derivation)
Signature Algorithms (ECDSA in Bitcoin, Ed25519 in newer chains)
Smart Contract Authentication (Verifying contract execution authority)
mindMaps.purpose: Shows how digital signatures are fundamental to blockchain functionality, connecting the cryptographic concept to practical decentralized system operations
